---
title: "Reproducible research in Price Statistics: Call for action!"
subtile: Overivew presented at the 2026 Ottawa Group (Warsaw, Poland)
bibliography: references.bib
authors:
- Serge Goussev
- Steve Martin
- Claude Lamboray
- Christophe Bontemps
- Tanya Flower
- Ben Hillman
- Caroline White
date: 2026-05-14
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: sky
    incremental: true
    menu:
      side: right
      width: wide
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: print
    fontsize: 26px
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

# Why should you care?

## Right now, it is a pain to replicate most research in price statistics

-   Say you found a cool methodological paper with an empirical section. Let's try to
    -   understand it / learn from it by trying it
    -   peer review it
    -   evaluate it on your own data and maybe use it in production
    -   extend it, there is a cool change that may make it better
-   Can we do any of the above right now in price statistics **without a large amount of effort**?

## Replication crisis is not new, it's actually well acknowledged in science

-   @10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124 and @baker20161 put the replication crisis on the map.

-   There have been a number of notable movements that have sought to help solve this problem. For example:

    -   **FAIR** - Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable - a set of principles for open science

    -   **Turing way** - a set of guides and processes to follow that

-   A lot of progress has been made! For example a recent survey of reproducibility in Economics (at least for journals with strict data sharing mandates and policies) reproducibility is as high as 85% [@brodeur2026reproducibility].

### Check out the references for more info

::: {#refs}
:::

## The bar for price statistics is lower, but it doesn't have to be this way!

-   In Price Statistics, we can adopt common practices to improve **replicability** (and ideally aim for **reproducibility**).

> We can move from a "trust me, it works on my data" toward "here is exactly how I did what I did, and here is everything you need to check my work."

-   The movement is actually already underway! For example increasing adoption of Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAP) is a step in the right direction!

-   What is left is to confirm **discipline specific research practices**.

# Who are we?

## A bit about us

-   As a workstream in the UN Task Team, we are a group of price statisticians from NSOs and research institutes around the world.

-   In 2024, we formed the FAIR/Reproducibility workstream to instill reproducibility within the discipline.

## How do we help?

-   We see two main blockers for reproducibility in price statistics:

    -   **Open datasets are few and far between, or are undocumented!** To get to reproducibilty, we all need access to high value open datasets. Without that, it is natural to default to internal data at our NSOs that is already processed, classified, and ready to go.

    -   **Unclear how to be reproducible!** There is a lot of guidance out there, but what works for our discipline? The learning curve is non-trivial – so why bother if the "how to do this" path isn't clear, and the mission isn't obvious?

-   We are working to solve both by:

    -   Deveoping and supporting a **curated catalogue** of good research datasets.

    -   Providing **guidance on practices** that apply to our discipline.

# What can you do about it?

## First off - prioritize reproducibility or replicability

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   We should aim for **reproducibility** for methodological studies – i.e. if the analysis (i.e. the code) is public and the data is open.

-   **Replicability** can be the backup – if the same data can't be used or to try other data with the same code

-   Without either, **generalizability** is the default. Generalizability however is:

    -   slower – it takes more time and resources to reach consensus

    -   sensitive to local patterns – an observed trend may be caused by the data or location, tough to tease out from an individual study

    -   publication bias is possible – it is natural to want to present findings that are appropriate for the data used
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Reproducibility quadrant](/docs/images/reproducibility-grid.svg){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::::

## Main objects of the research

![](/docs/images/reserarch-objects-deeper-dive-1-research.drawio.svg)

## Maturity model

### Maturity level 0

### Maturity level 1

### Maturity level 2

## Structure your research compendium

![](/docs/images/price-stats-exemplar.svg)

## Choose a licence

## Process the input data (or document it)

## Sensitive data

## Publish your data or your compendium

## Metadata of the research process

![](/docs/images/reserarch-objects-deeper-dive-2-citation.drawio.svg)

## What about the data?

![](/docs/images/data-catalogue-idea.svg)

## Are you convinced?

Great!

-   Use open data
-   Follow our guides (or that of others that we reference)
-   Let us know if we're missing anything
-   Recommend datasets if you see some missing
-   Cite our guide!

> Goussev, S., Martin, S., Lamboray, C., Bontemps, C., Flower, T., Hillman, B., White, C., & Mehroff, J. (2026). Price Statistics Reproducibility Project (v0.1). Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19779579

## Not convinced?

Tell us what we're missing? What would make it easy for you develop your project in a reproducible way?

> should we do a survey?

## Our next steps

-   Proprietary datasets - add to the catalog and provide guidance
-   Work with CPI EG and OG to make reproducibitliy simpler for conference participants
-   Flush out advanced topics not currently flushed out
